  :                            251 
00:002C87 60B000              +252 readq	la	queue:head
00:002C88 C60C8B              +253 	jza	readqz
00:002C89 68C000              +254 	lb	0, a
00:002C8A 28B000              +255 	sb	queue:head
00:002C8B 350000              +256 readqz	lret	0
  :                            257 
  :                            258         $if      QSIMULTANEOUS
  :                            259 
  :                            260 .	descriptors are short-term on two chains
  :                            261 .	so that TCP segments awaiting ACK
  :                            262 .	can be on the interface queue and the
  :                            263 .	connection's ACK wait queue at the same time
  :                            264 
  :                            265 .	TCP uses chain link descriptor:next and so does
  :                            266 .	almost all the t77.4 protocol suite
  :                            267 
  :                            268 .	interface queues use chain link descriptor:ifql
  :                            269 
  :                            270 .	ifql is a reuse of descriptor:readpoint which
  :                            271 .	which points TCP past consumed / duplicate
  :                            272 .	payload in incoming segments
  :                            273 
  :                            274 .	in high-volume space where certain TCP ports
  :                            275 .	have private buffer pools [HVBANK], the buffer
  :                            276 .	and its descriptor are in the same bank
  :                            277 
  :                            278 .	the descriptor is always located by a chain
  :                            279 .	head or previous descriptor pointing forward
  :                            280 .	to it
  :                            281 
  :                            282 .	in the private buffer bank arrangement TCP
  :                            283 .	inbound segment descriptors are located with
  :                            284 .	the pointer previous:sobdi.previous:next
  :                            285 
  :                            286 .	descriptor:sobdi is part of a next pointer
  :                            287 .	and does not concern the buffer of the descriptor
  :                            288 
  :                            289 .	sobdi is a reuse of apdata, which points to
  :                            290 .	the trailing edge of tcp segment header /
  :                            291 .	leading edge of tcp segment payload. apdata
  :                            292 .	is only needed during consumption and
  :                            293 .	construction of datagrams, and in high-volume
  :                            294 .	private buffer arrangements is replaced
  :                            295 .	during input and output by sobdi, which is
  :                            296 .	the bank base of the socket's private buffer 
  :                            297 .	pool
  :                            298 
  :                            299 .	during operations within TCP the bank containing
  :                            300 .	the segment is in address space whenever the socket
  :                            301 .	is in address space	
  :                            302 
  :                            303 .	high-volume private buffer pools are only used
  :                            304 .	in some TCP traffic which is expected to be of
  :                            305 .	extreme volume or confidential. The private buffer
  :                            306 .	arrangement enables millions of transmitted segments
  :                            307 .	awaiting remote acknowledgement to remain buffered
  :                            308 
  :                            309 .	interface device drivers drop transmitted datagrams
  :                            310 .	from protocols which do not resend
  :                            311 
  :                            312 .	TCP flags bit 23 of descriptor:bytes and interface
  :                            313 .	drivers do not drop those buffers after transmission
  :                            314 
  :                            315 
00:002C8C 70B000              +316 q2if	tz	queue:head
00:002C8D B60C92              +317 	j	q2ifz
00:002C8E 20B000              +318 	sa	queue:head
00:002C8F 20B001              +319 	sa	queue:tail
00:002C90 30C007              +320 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002C91 350000              +321 	lret	0
  :                            322 
00:002C92 68B001              +323 q2ifz	lb	queue:tail
00:002C93 20D007              +324 	sa	descriptor:ifql, b
00:002C94 20B001              +325 	sa	queue:tail
00:002C95 30C007              +326 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002C96 350000              +327 	lret	0
  :                            328 
00:002C97 60B000              +329 readqif	la	queue:head
00:002C98 C60C9B              +330 	jza	rqifz
00:002C99 68C007              +331 	lb	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002C9A 28B000              +332 	sb	queue:head
00:002C9B 350000              +333 rqifz	lret	0
  :                            334 
  :                            335 
  :                            336 	$if	HVBANK
  :                            337 
  :                            338 .	these macros work in A$BUFFB which is TCP outgoing
  :                            339 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            340 .	to manage any other memory window 
  :                            341 
  :                            342 .	the descriptor + buffer returned at hvreadifq are
  :                            343 .	placed in memory window A$BUFFB
  :                            344 
  :                            345 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2ifhv are not
  :                            346 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space, but
  :                            347 .	their long address is still in a:b
  :                            348 
  :                            349 q2ifhv	tz	1, y
  :                            350 	j	q2ifhv_tail
  :                            351 	ds	hvqueue:head 
  :                            352 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            353 	lret	0
  :                            354 q2ifhv_tail
  :                            355 	dpush	a
  :                            356 	dpush	a
  :                            357 	dl	hvqueue:tail		. get qtail word offset pointer
  :                            358 	sabr	A$BUFFB			. get *qtail in address space
  :                            359 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b	. write new qtail bank pointer
  :                            360 	pop	descriptor:ifql,b	. write new qtail word offset pointer
  :                            361 	pop	a			. take 2nd copy descriptor pointer
  :                            362 	pop	b			. from the internal stack
  :                            363 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            364 	lret	0
  :                            365 
  :                            366 hvreadqif
  :                            367 	dl	hvqueue:head
  :                            368 	jzb	hvreadqifz		. queue empty
  :                            369 	sabr	A$BUFFB			. first descriptor into address space
  :                            370 	push	descriptor:sobdi, b	. next pointer to queue head
  :                            371 	push	descriptor:ifql, b
  :                            372 	pop	1, y
  :                            373 	pop	0, y
  :                            374 hvreadqifz
  :                            375 	lret	0
  :                            376 
  :                            377 .	these macros use descriptor:next pointer like readq
  :                            378 .	and q2, and are used in upper protocols
  :                            379 
  :                            380 .	descriptor:sobdi is the containing bank pointer of
  :                            381 .	the descriptor + buffer at descriptor:next
  :                            382 
  :                            383 .	these macros work in A$BUFFA which is TCP incoming
  :                            384 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            385 .	to manage any other memory window
  :                            386 
  :                            387 .	the descriptor + buffer returned from hvreadq are
  :                            388 .	based in window A$BUFFA
  :                            389 
  :                            390 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2hv are not
  :                            391 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space
  :                            392 .	but their long address is still in a:b
  :                            393 
  :                            394 q2hv	tz	1, y
  :                            395 	j	q2hv_tail
  :                            396 	ds	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            397 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            398 	lret	0
  :                            399 q2hv_tail
  :                            400 	dpush	a
  :                            401 	dpush	a
  :                            402 	dl	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            403 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            404 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b
  :                            405 	pop	descriptor:next, b
  :                            406 	pop	a
  :                            407 	pop	b
  :                            408 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            409 	lret	0
  :                            410 
  :                            411 hvreadq	dl	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            412 	jzb	hvreadqifz
  :                            413 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            414 	push	descriptor:next_bdi, b
  :                            415 	push	descriptor:next, b
  :                            416 	pop	1, y
  :                            417 	pop	0, y
  :                            418 	lret	0
  :                            419 
  :                            420 
  :                            421 	$endif	HVBANK
  :                            422 	$endif	QSIMULTANEOUS

  :                            98 ethernet_arp	$tree
  :                            99 
  :                            100 .	ARP for
  :                            101 .		48-bit physical addresses
  :                            102 .	and	32-bit protocol addresses
  :                            103 
  :                            104 .	this may be the only ARP needed
  :                            105 .	if another ARP is needed
  :                            106 .	for different size addresses
  :                            107 .	include arp.msm again
  :                            108 
  :                            109 .	netspace	$tree
  :                            110 .	SETTINGS	$set	values
  :                            111 .			$include arp
  :                            112 .			$root
  :                            113 .
  :                            114 .	whatever storage bank that is in
  :                            115 .	needs four vectors at the front
  :                            116 
  :                            117 .	netspa_arp_rx* $vector	netspace:arp_rx
  :                            118 .	netspa_arp_reso* $vector netspace:resolve
  :                            119 .	netspa_arp_xmit* $vector netspace:xmit
  :                            120 .	xarp_netspace*	$vector	netspace:xarp
  :                            121 
  :                            122 .	these vectors go in the interface descriptor
  :                            123 
  :                            124 .	this_if:arp_rx
  :                            125 .	this_if:arp_tx
  :                            126 .	this_if:arp_resolve
  :                            127 .	this_if:xarp
  :                            128 
  :                            129 .		ql	(netspa_arp_rx, netspa_arp_tx, netspa_arp_reso, xarp_netspace:q)
  :                            130 .		qs	this_if:arp_rx
  :                            131 
  :                            132 .	this would usually happen in ifconfigure.msm  where 
  :                            133 .	the vector pointer to the routine for constructing
  :                            134 .	link layer header is also written to this_if:write_llh
  :                            135 
  :                            136 
  :                            137 .	the settings and and generation of ethernet ARP follow here
  :                            138 
  :                            139 
  :                            140 ARP_STORE $set	50	. however many ARP cache rows are required
  :                            141 
  :                            142 HTYPE	$set	ETHERNET_SPACE
  :                            143 HLEN	$set	6
  :                            144 PLEN	$set	4
  :                            145 PROTOCOL_SUITE	$set	0800
  :                            146 LINK_PROTOCOL	$set	0806
  :                            147 
  :                            148 ARP_XMIT	$set	arp_ether_xmit
  :                            149

  :                            168 
  :                            169 fp_multiply
00:005129 FCF000              +170 	lc	heavy	left,,i	right,,i
00:00512A FCF009              +170 
00:00512B FCF002              +170 
00:00512C 3E00A2              +170 
00:00512D 38000F              +170 
00:00512E 1E0129              +171 	jdr	fp_multiply
00:00512F B60152              +172 	j	fp_edit
  :                            173 
  :                            174 
  :                            175 .	the other 3 functions process two 192-bit inputs
  :                            176 
  :                            177 .	but 192-bit divide must test 
  :                            178 .	whether the divisor is represented in 1 or 2 floating numbers
  :                            179 
  :                            180 .	a 96-bit number sounds big
  :                            181 .	but if the divisor exponent is < 0 or > 31 decimal equivalent
  :                            182 .	then the 2nd divisor floating number is nonzero
  :                            183 .	for now deliver an indicative result in this case
  :                            184 .	by rounding both dividend and divisor to 96 bits
  :                            185 .	(adding their major and minor parts) and delivering a 96-bit quotient
  :                            186 
  :                            187 .	if the divisor is only one floating number (minor part is zero)
  :                            188 .	deliver a 192-bit quotient from a 192-bit dividend 
  :                            189

  :                            414 .	when you first store a managed object
  :                            415 .	you store its rules and the address
  :                            416 .	where its data is
  :                            417 
  :                            418 .	when you subsequently store the same
  :                            419 .	managed object, you write new data
  :                            420 .	at the address in the descriptor
  :                            421 .	and the rules initially stored apply
  :                            422 
00:00541F 60F009              +423 	la	oclass

  :                            464 
00:005440 D50004              +465 	ana, xi	4
00:005441 7E0447              +466 	jc	mib_store_long_range?
00:005442 B6044D              +467 	j	mib_store_range?
  :                            468 
  :                            469 .	the method of having signed and unsigned range checks
  :                            470 .	without noticing too much
  :                            471 
  :                            472 .	firstly, if the high-order bit of the upper and lower limit
  :                            473 .	are identical, just do unsigned compares, and that will give
  :                            474 .	the right result whether the candidate integer is meant to
  :                            475 .	be signed or not
  :                            476 
  :                            477 .	secondly, if the high order bits of the two limits are 
  :                            478 .	different, then
  :                            479 
  :                            480 .	if the upper limit interpreted as unsigned is greater than
  :                            481 .	the lower limit interpreted as unsigned (the high order bit
  :                            482 .	of the upper limit is on, and not the high order bit of the
  :                            483 .	lower limit), just do an unsigned compare, because the
  :                            484 .	low-high difference cannot be a negative difference between
  :                            485 .	two signed integers. It can only be a lower limit in the
  :                            486 .	lower half of the number space, and an upper limit in the
  :                            487 .	upper half of the unsigned number space
  :                            488 
  :                            489 .	in all the above cases, the candidate integer is within
  :                            490 .	the range tested, if both carry is generated from the
  :                            491 .	subtraction of the lower limit from the integer, and also
  :                            492 .	carry is generated from the subtraction of the integer from
  :                            493 .	the upper limit
  :                            494 
  :                            495 .	this depends upon carry being consistent with adding the
  :                            496 .	minus of one number to the plus of another
  :                            497 
  :                            498 .	RTA1 is logically correct for this. The result and carried
  :                            499 .	result of
  :                            500 
  :                            501 .		ana	THIS
  :                            502 .		anb	THAT
  :                            503 .		dan	THE_OTHER
  :                            504 
  :                            505 
  :                            506 .	are identical to the result and carried result of
  :                            507 
  :                            508 .		aa	MINUS_THIS
  :                            509 .		ab	MINUS_THAT
  :                            510 .		da	MINUS_THE_OTHER
  :                            511 
  :                            512 .	carry$ set after ana anb dan means the unsigned number in
  :                            513 .	the accumulators was at least as large as the number
  :                            514 .	subtracted from it
  :                            515 .		
  :                            516 .
  :                            517 .	if the high-order bit of the lower limit is on and not the
  :                            518 .	high order bit of the upper limit, this is a signed range
  :                            519 .	which includes the value zero between its upper and lower
  :                            520 .	bounds. Then the candidate integer is within the range
  :                            521 .	tested if both the result of the integer minus the lower limit
  :                            522 .	is of positive polarity, and also the result of the upper limit
  :                            523 .	minus the integer is positive polarity
  :                            524 
  :                            525 .	polarity (and zero) are tested in the registers holding the
  :                            526 .	results, because the only condition flag in RTA1 is carry$
  :                            527 
  :                            528 .	examples of instructions testing results other than carry are
  :                            529 
  :                            530 .		jpa	here
  :                            531 .		jnb	there
  :                            532 .		jdz	everywhere
  :                            533 .		jzb	b_is_zero
  :                            534 .		jpe	a_has_even_parity
  :                            535 
  :                            536 .		tp	location_a_few_million
  :                            537 .		j	the_word_at_location_a_few_million_is_negative
  :                            538 
  :                            539 .		tz	2, sp
  :                            540 .		j	the_third_register_in_the_internal_stack_is_nonzero
  :                            541 
  :                            542 
  :                            543 mib_store_long_range!

  :                            128 .	the parts to pull out are
  :                            129 .	_________________________
  :                            130 
  :                            131 .	30LL	binding and length		00..7F 81:80..FF 82:0100..FFFF
  :                            132 .	0201vv	SNMP version	00 = v 1
  :                            133 
  :                            134 .	then for v 1 at least...
  :                            135 
  :                            136 .	04LLalpha community string
  :                            137 
  :                            138 .	AxLL	command byte pdu length		00..7F 81:80..FF 82:0100..FFFF
  :                            139 .	160 = get 161 = getnext 163 = set
  :                            140 
  :                            141 .	0204xxxxxxxx	request ID typically 4 bytes
  :                            142 
  :                            143 .	020100	status
  :                            144 .	020100	error index
  :                            145 
  :                            146 .	30LL	binding and length		00..7F 81:80..FF 82:0100..FFFF
  :                            147 .	30LL-2	maybe another one nested
  :                            148 
  :                            149 .	06LLxxxxxx...0500	oids with NULLtype zeroL for get / getnext
  :                            150 .	06LLxxxxxx...4103yyyyyy	oids with typeLL / value for set
  :                            151 
  :                            152 .	strings may be > 127 bytes, length is encoded
  :                            153 .						00..7F 81:80..FF 82:0100..FFFF
  :                            154 
  :                            155 .	______________________________________________________________
  :                            156 
  :                            157 .	if the command is not A0 A1 A3 the request is dropped silently
  :                            158 
  :                            159 .	if the bindings lengths don't figure with the containing lengths
  :                            160 .	the request is dropped silently
  :                            161 
  :                            162 .	if community string is unknown the request is dropped silently
  :                            163 
  :                            164 
  :                            165 .	the response is as the request except
  :                            166 .	_____________________________________
  :                            167 
  :                            168 .	the command byte or response is SNMP_RESPONSE A2 162
  :                            169 
  :                            170 .	the response to successful get / getnext contains
  :                            171 .	a value encoding, not NULLtype/zeroL 0500
  :                            172 
  :                            173 
  :                            174 .	if anything has went wrong
  :                            175 
  :                            176 .		status is the error
  :                            177 
  :                            178 .		1 = something was too large in some way		tooBig
  :                            179 .		2 = that does not exist				noSuchName
  :                            180 .		3 = value wrong type or out of range		badValue
  :                            181 .		4 = object may not be wrote			readOnly
  :                            182 .		5 = something else was wrong			genErr
  :                            183 
  :                            184 .		error index indicates the [1st] oid binding which is in error
  :                            185 .		relative 1, 1 = 1st oid binding in the PDU
  :                            186 
  :                            187 .	the plan is
  :                            188 .	take all the actions and table the results as BER templates
  :                            189 
  :                            190 .	if anything is wrong, send the bindings back as received
  :                            191 .	with error-code set to the mistake
  :                            192 .	and error-index pointing to the first binding which is in error
  :                            193 
  :                            194 .	(no further ones need be scanned if that happens)
  :                            195 .	this means the update values are returned but not actioned
  :                            196 
  :                            197 .	and the read-requests are returned with null values as received
  :                            198 
  :                            199 .	even multiple get-nexts can be sensibly answered on this principle
  :                            200 
  :                            201 .	and if everything has went right, produce the BER-encoded results
  :                            202 
  :                            203
